text: Hi, Dr. Charles Chabert, urologist and director of The Prostate Clinic. The following video continues on our series looking at surgical technique.
The following video is taken during a robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy, so a form of treatment for localized prostate cancer.
The surgeon performs bladder neck dissection during a robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy, carefully separating the bladder neck from surrounding tissues.
The prostate is visible at the top of the screen with the dorsal venous complex already suture ligated.
The surgeon applies traction in a cephalad direction on the anterior aspect of the bladder during the robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy.
Gentle traction is applied to tease open and separate the anterior bladder neck, progressing towards the supraprostatic urethra in the midline during the robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon carefully continues the dissection to identify the lateral aspect of the urethra during the robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection to achieve complete circumnavigation of the supraprostatic urethra, ensuring thorough exposure of this anatomical structure.
The surgeon punches through the posterior tissue at the posterior bladder neck, creating an opening visible in the surgical field.
This gentleman had a nice upgrade to prostate cancer. It was anteriorly placed.
And so this gentleman is having things set up to perform a bilateral nerve sparing radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon completes the dissection around the urethra and proceeds to divide the supraprostatic urethra, revealing the catheter within the surgical field.
The catheter is retracted and secured, then lifted up onto the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon manipulates the bladder, allowing it to drop away from the surgical field, aiming to maintain a small bladder neck during the procedure.
In my opinion, that facilitates two things. It facilitates the anastomosis itself. but it also facilitates and allows early return of urinary control.
The surgeon dissects the posterior plane down to reveal the midline structures, including the vasa deferentia, and more laterally, the seminal vesicles.
The surgeon secures the wings of the bladder using a hemlock clip, ensuring the clip is positioned above the origin of the neurovascular bundle.
The surgeon performs the same procedure on the patient's right side, grasping and elevating the right vas deferens, which is now visible among the posterior structures.
The surgeon controls the vas deferens using a surgical instrument, releasing the surrounding tissue to expose the structure further.
The surgeon performs anterior retraction and uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to identify and mobilize the right seminal vesicle down to its tip.
The surgeon uses the ProGrasp instrument to hold the tip of the right seminal vesicle. A clip is applied around the right vas and the vasal artery, which is located posterior to the vas.
The surgeon identifies an artery heading towards the tip of the seminal vesicle and applies a hemlock clip to control it in an athermal fashion.
The surgeon performs the same procedure on the patient's left side, using the ProGrasp to manipulate and elevate the left vas and associated posterior structures.

The surgeon elevates the left vas deferens, retracting it towards the anterior abdominal wall. The left seminal vesicle is identified and exposed. Monopolar scissors and bipolar instruments are used to elevate the seminal vesicle, facilitating the exposure of the left vas deferens, which is then clipped and divided.
The surgeon applies a clip to secure the artery at the tip of the left seminal vesicle, following further mobilization achieved by dividing the left vas deferens.
The surgeon mobilizes the end of the seminal vesicle and retracts it upwards towards the anterior abdominal wall, enhancing exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon exposes Denonvilliers' fascia, a biolaminar structure visible between the anterior wall of the rectum and the posterior aspect of the prostate.
